metadata.source_title: A bitter aftertaste looms from a blind 5G auction
metadata.tags: Headlines & Insights, Technologie, Media & Telecom, 5G, Duurzaamheid
metadata.author: Kamalika Patra
metadata.description: The government is considering partially ‘blind’ auctioning the 3.5 gigahertz 5G frequency next year. It hopes this will channel higher proceeds into the treasury. High frequency costs, however, mean less room for necessary network investment. That could hinder the growth of the Dutch economy and the circular transition.
metadata.parent_text: Owners of mobile networks are [concerned](https://fd.nl/ondernemen/1373591/mobiele-operators-vrezen-te-hoge-kosten-nieuwe-5g-veiling-oqb1ca9Nlm1S) that the new auction model will result in sky‑high licence costs at the expense of network investment. In last year’s auction of lower 5G frequencies, all bids were transparent to participants and the three highest bidders ultimately each secured the same amount of bandwidth at the same price. In the coming blind auction, three bandwidths will be allocated without candidates having insight into each other’s bids. In theory, that would yield higher proceeds because bidders are prompted to submit their best offer straight away. Network owners therefore recently [argued](https://www.telecompaper.com/nieuws/mnos-pleiten-bij-tweede-kamer-tegen-plannen-35-ghz-veiling--1371854) in the House of Representatives for an investment‑friendly auction model. They argue that rising licence costs ultimately lead to higher economic and social costs. Additional investment in network infrastructure is crucial to unlock the full potential of 5G. The increase in speed and data capacity when upgrading to the 5G network is achieved not only through higher frequencies and hardware adjustments, as with previous generation upgrades, but also by replacing old antennas with new ‘smart’ antennas1. These Multiple‑Input Multiple‑Output antennas can actively track devices and direct an isolated signal at devices that are processing data at that moment. This reduces energy consumption and streamlines data transmission for ultra‑fast mobile internet with versatile, intelligent applications in industry. Experience shows that higher costs easily lead to higher prices for end users, which holds back their technological development. The sectors where those higher auction costs weigh most heavily are industry and trade, where 5G can deliver major system improvements. For example, the development of smart factories in industry can benefit greatly from fast networks. Among other things, machine‑to‑machine communication makes it possible to collect and analyse data in real time to optimise production processes. In wholesale and retail, fault diagnostics could also be carried out continuously and intelligently, so less delay occurs in identifying causes of malfunctions in complex sorting processes2.

metadata.parent_text: Owners of mobile networks are [concerned](https://fd.nl/ondernemen/1373591/mobiele-operators-vrezen-te-hoge-kosten-nieuwe-5g-veiling-oqb1ca9Nlm1S) that the new auction model will result in sky‑high licence costs at the expense of network investment. In last year’s auction of lower 5G frequencies, all bids were transparent to participants and the three highest bidders ultimately each secured the same amount of bandwidth at the same price. In the coming blind auction, three bandwidths will be allocated without candidates having insight into each other’s bids. In theory, that would yield higher proceeds because bidders are prompted to submit their best offer straight away. Network owners therefore recently [argued](https://www.telecompaper.com/nieuws/mnos-pleiten-bij-tweede-kamer-tegen-plannen-35-ghz-veiling--1371854) in the House of Representatives for an investment‑friendly auction model. They argue that rising licence costs ultimately lead to higher economic and social costs. Additional investment in network infrastructure is crucial to unlock the full potential of 5G. The increase in speed and data capacity when upgrading to the 5G network is achieved not only through higher frequencies and hardware adjustments, as with previous generation upgrades, but also by replacing old antennas with new ‘smart’ antennas1. These Multiple‑Input Multiple‑Output antennas can actively track devices and direct an isolated signal at devices that are processing data at that moment. This reduces energy consumption and streamlines data transmission for ultra‑fast mobile internet with versatile, intelligent applications in industry. Experience shows that higher costs easily lead to higher prices for end users, which holds back their technological development. The sectors where those higher auction costs weigh most heavily are industry and trade, where 5G can deliver major system improvements. For example, the development of smart factories in industry can benefit greatly from fast networks. Among other things, machine‑to‑machine communication makes it possible to collect and analyse data in real time to optimise production processes. In wholesale and retail, fault diagnostics could also be carried out continuously and intelligently, so less delay occurs in identifying causes of malfunctions in complex sorting processes2.

metadata.parent_text: Less delay, lower energy consumption, greater reliability and wider reach in deploying 5G are the indispensable [characteristics](https://www.mckinsey.com/~/media/mckinsey/industries/advanced%20electronics/our%20insights/the%205g%20era%20new%20horizons%20for%20advanced%20electronics%20and%20industrial%20companies/the-5g-era-new-horizons-for-advanced-electronics-and-industrial-companies.pdf) of the successful production processes of the future. All these smart capabilities, however, cannot be fully utilised if network investment fails to materialise. That affects the Netherlands’ competitive position, especially as other countries are already [actively working with 5G](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/hulp-van-5g-bij-verhoging-arbeidsproductiviteit.html). An outdated technological infrastructure leaves traces in the long term; essential infrastructure forms the basis for further technological development. A substandard 5G network will readily suppress economic growth. And industry and trade are precisely the sectors with the largest added value in the Dutch economy. Industrial activities account for as much as 22 per cent of all added value and trade accounts for 12 per cent3.
